retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Alveolar Osteitis
retrieved_docs.content: Alveolar osteitis is a clinical diagnosis and does not require laboratory studies or radiographic examination. A thorough history and physical exam are sufficient to diagnose this postoperative complication. However, panoramic radiographs can be performed if there is a concern for a retained tooth fragment within the alveolar socket. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Osteosarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: The features seen on plain film radiographs vary depending on the destructive nature of the tumor and the degree of remineralization. Often, a lesion with irregular, poorly defined margins is observed. Several classical signs may be noted. They are not pathognomonic for osteosarcoma and are not always present. Codman’s triangle is an effect caused by the elevation of the periosteum over the lesion in a tent-like manner. The classical ‘sun-ray’ appearance describes the invasion of the periosteum by thin spicules of bony tumors in an outward direction. Garrington’s sign describes widening of the periodontal ligament and inferior alveolar nerve canal. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiographs can show the extent of the infection and help evaluate areas that are not obvious to visual evaluation. These include intraoral periapical radiographs, bitewing radiographs, and panoramic radiographs. These radiographs help to identify the extent of dental caries, the presence of a periapical abscess, alveolar bone loss concerning periodontitis, and third molar eruptions. The dental practitioner’s physical examination must confirm all radiographic evidence for a full evaluation and accurate diagnosis. CT scans and MRIs help assess deep-space infections and osteomyelitis. Cone-beam CT is becoming increasingly common in clinical settings to better visualize areas of oral infections. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Alveolar Osteitis
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam demonstrates expected postoperative changes with a partial or complete loss of the blood clot within the alveolar socket. Though it can be difficult to appreciate on visual inspection, exposed alveolar bone is the classic physical exam finding of alveolar osteitis. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Osteoradionecrosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation should include: Review radiotherapy and dental record Biopsy of wound Panographic Radiographs CT of mandible Stage the disease Rule out recurrence/persistence of primary malignancy or new secondary malignancy by getting a biopsy of the wound. [9] [10] [11] [8] Imaging studies aid in the diagnosis and quantify the extent of disease. CT findings: Cortical bone disruption with mixed lysis and sclerosis can be seen. Pathologic fractures can also be seen. Panographic radiograph findings: Plain radiographs are useful for screening but can underestimate the extent of lesions. Lytic areas with ill-defined, non-sclerotic borders can be seen. Gather record of radiotherapy and review treatment method, radiation portal, and total dose. Marx Staging of ORN is the most common staging system in use. It is based on response to hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO).

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoradionecrosis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoradionecrosis of the jaw (ORN) is a late effect of radiation therapy. Previously irradiated head and neck tissues become hypovascular and hypoxic. If in the irradiated field, the mandible may develop aseptic, avascular necrosis which can lead to infection, tooth loss, and even pathological fracture of the jaw. Osteoradionecrosis is rarely seen in patients who received less than 6000 centiGrays (cGy) of radiation and may occur years or even decades after radiation is concluded. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The need for radiographs should be determined based on whether the information obtained will affect management and whether radiation exposure is warranted. In dental trauma cases, radiographs are typically recommended since root fractures may not always present with clinical signs and symptoms and may otherwise be missed.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoradionecrosis(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoradionecrosis occurs in patients who have been previously exposed to ionizing radiation to treat head and neck cancers. The radiation leaves the bony and soft tissues with poor vascularity, resulting in a picture of avascular necrosis in the mandible. Patients may develop areas of exposed bone in the mouth and resultant loss of teeth and supporting structures. The resulting chronic infections can lead to osteomyelitis and soft tissue orocutaneous fistulae. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic assessment is not pathognomonic and may vary. It will show a radiolucent lesion, which may be small around the root of a tooth or large, involving most of the jaw, resorbing the cortical plate and adjacent teeth. For small lesions, plain radiographs may suffice. For large, aggressively destructive lesions, 3-dimensional CT imaging will provide a more complete picture of the bony destruction. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Dentinogenesis imperfecta is identified on clinical examination. Teeth appear amber, brown/blue, or opalescent brown in color, depending on the extent of the dysplastic dentin. [26] Radiographically, crowns appear bulbous with small to obliterated pulp chambers, and roots are often narrow with small pulp canals. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Parry-Romberg Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Dental Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: To  monitor progressive deformity of the jaw bones and teeth abnormalities by serial orthopantomograms and photographs.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Biopsies -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Hard Tissue: Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Bony lesions of the maxilla and mandible are characterized by their radiographic appearance. Many of these lesions arise from cells of odontogenic origin or cell lineages associated with tooth development, as teeth are the main structures within the gnathic complex. On imaging, bony lesions can be radiopaque (hyperdense), radiolucent (hypodense), or present with a mixed picture. Radiopaque lesions are common and often represent a subset of conditions that do not require biopsy. Single areas of asymptomatic hyperdensity with no destruction or expansion are often found, and the differential diagnosis includes idiopathic osteosclerosis, cemento-osseous dysplasia, and condensing osteitis, usually from a tooth cavity that creates a low-grade inflammatory stimulus over time.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Osteochondroma
retrieved_docs.content: Plain film radiograph of osteochondroma has pathognomonic features, specifically continuity of the medullary space with the underlying bone. Three classical appearances have been described. Firstly, an enlarged condyle with anteromedial extension into the lateral pterygoid. [44] Second is the ‘shredded flag’ appearance with a mass arising from the lateral aspect of the condyle. Finally, is a lesion originating from the medial aspect of the condyle, better appreciated on CT imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Alveolar Osteitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Post-operative imaging, such as a panoramic radiograph, could rule out remaining tooth fragments or bony sequestra. Markers of fibrinolysis have been investigated for risk prediction in cardiovascular disease but are not helpful in a clinical setting with AO. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Alveolar Osteitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Vital signs are stable, and the patient is most commonly afebrile. The pain is accompanied by a totally or partially broken down blood clot in the alveolar socket. Exposed bone secondary to this fibrinolysis is the typical clinical presentation but may be difficult to visualize on examination. [3]

This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: Intrakavitární radioterapie is a specialized form of radiation therapy that targets lesions within body cavities; the two terms are closely related and may be considered synonymous in clinical practice, reflecting the same therapeutic approach of delivering ionizing radiation to treat intracavitary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the clinical evaluation of patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of secondary lesions indicates disease spread or metastatic involvement, which is essential for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies. Recognizing secondary lesions therefore plays a critical role in the comprehensive management of bone cancer.
